data.title: Chemung Financial (CHMG) Margin Compression Versus 24.3% Prior-Year Net Margin Tests Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Chemung Financial (CHMG) has just wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter total revenue of US$29.4 million and basic EPS of US$1.61, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$7.7 million. Over the past few quarters, the company has seen total revenue range from US$24.6 million to US$29.4 million while basic EPS moved between US$1.24 in Q4 2024 and US$1.61 in Q4 2025, with a sharp loss in Q2 2025 breaking that pattern. Taken together with the latest print, the focus is on how sustainably Chemung Financial can manage its margins from here.

See our full analysis for Chemung Financial.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely followed narratives about Chemung Financial to see which views the data supports and which ones are challenged by the reported figures.

Margins and Costs Move Together

  • Across 2025, Chemung Financial's reported net interest margin ranged from 2.96% in Q1 to 3.45% in Q3, while the cost to income ratio moved between 61.18% and 65.69% over the same quarters.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that margin and cost metrics in 2025, such as the 3.45% net interest margin and 61.18% cost to income ratio in Q3, line up with the idea of a traditional community bank that relies on spread income and disciplined expense control. However, the trailing net profit margin of 16.7% versus 24.3% a year earlier shows that recent profitability is not as strong as that optimistic framing suggests.
    • Bulls who like the long history and traditional model still have to reconcile that lower 16.7% trailing margin with earlier levels near 24.3% cited in the data.
    • At the same time, basic EPS in the individual 2025 quarters ranged from a loss of US$1.34 in Q2 to profits of US$1.26 to US$1.62 in the other quarters, which shows that the bank's earnings pattern has been uneven even within a single year.

Loan Book Growth with Mixed Credit Signals

  • Total loans in the periodical data stepped from US$2,028.95 million in Q3 2024 to US$2,202.36 million in Q3 2025, while non performing loans moved in a band between US$7.76 million and US$10.55 million across the reported quarters.
  • Critics who take a more bearish stance may focus on the fact that non performing loans sat at US$10.55 million in Q3 2024 and US$9.88 million in Q1 2025, even as total loans increased. This means the growing loan book and the exposure figures given in the data leave room for bears to argue that asset quality needs to be watched closely.
    • The combination of higher total loans of over US$2.2b and non performing loans still around US$7.76 million to US$9.88 million in several quarters keeps credit costs in the conversation for cautious investors.
    • Because net interest margin in those same quarters was between 2.72% and 3.45%, any shift in non performing loans within that US$7.76 million to US$10.55 million range could have a direct impact on future profitability metrics that bears are likely to track closely.

Valuation Gap Between P/E and DCF Fair Value

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 18.8x with a share price of US$59.11, while the supplied DCF fair value is US$133.07 and the cited analyst price target is US$64.25, and the modelled comparison suggests the market price is about 55.6% below that DCF fair value.
  • What is relevant for a bullish angle is that forecasts in the dataset point to earnings growth of about 38.6% per year and revenue growth of about 19% per year, alongside a 2.3% dividend yield. However, the trailing P/E of 18.8x is higher than both the 12.4x peer average and the 11.8x US Banks industry, so the case for upside rests on those growth forecasts and the DCF fair value of US$133.07 being more important than the premium multiple on past earnings.
    • Supporters of the stock can point to trailing 12 month net income of US$15.10 million on revenue of US$90.67 million and the 16.7% profit margin as the earnings base that the 38.6% forecast growth rate is applied to.
    • Skeptics, on the other hand, can counter that the same P/E of 18.8x, which is above both peer and industry averages, already prices in some of that forecast growth even before any change in the DCF fair value or the US$64.25 price target is reflected in the share price of US$59.11.

data.title: Boeing (BA) Q4 US$8.1b Profit Tests Bearish Narratives On Persistent Losses
data.description:
Boeing (BA) closed out FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$23.9b and basic EPS of US$10.59, alongside net income of US$8.1b, putting a profitable quarter against what has been a loss making year on a trailing basis. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$15.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$5.46 in Q4 2024 to US$23.9b and EPS of US$10.59 in Q4 2025, while trailing twelve month figures sit at US$89.5b of revenue and basic EPS of US$2.49 with net income of US$1.9b. For investors, the swing between quarterly profits and prior losses sets up a close look at how durable these margins really are.

See our full analysis for Boeing.

With the latest earnings on the table, the next step is to see how these margins and profit trends line up with the widely held narratives around Boeing's recovery and longer term outlook.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

US$8.1b profit caps a volatile year

  • Q4 net income of US$8.1b contrasts sharply with losses earlier in FY 2025, where Boeing reported a loss of US$5.4b in Q3 and smaller losses in Q2 and Q1, while the trailing twelve month net income sits at a much lower US$1.9b.
  • Bears who focus on Boeing's recent loss making history and growing losses over the past five years will point out that, even after a strong quarter, trailing basic EPS of US$2.49 and prior trailing twelve month losses of US$10.2b at Q3 keep the longer term record firmly tied to past losses, not a clean earnings recovery yet.
    • Critics highlight that Q4's US$8.1b profit has to be weighed against earlier FY 2025 quarterly losses of US$697m and US$123m, which still shape the full year picture.
    • The trailing shift from a loss of US$11.7b at Q1 2025 to a profit of US$1.9b by Q4 supports the idea of progress, but does not erase the five year trend of increasing losses flagged in the risk summary.

Revenue climbs to US$89.5b over the year

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, revenue has moved from US$66.5b at Q4 2024 to US$89.5b at Q4 2025, with quarterly revenue running between US$19.5b and US$23.9b through FY 2025.
  • Supporters of a more optimistic view argue that this higher revenue base, alongside forecasts for 9.3% annual revenue growth, gives earnings room to improve even though that projected growth rate sits below the 10.6% forecast for the wider US market.
    • What stands out for that bullish angle is that trailing revenue of US$89.5b is materially higher than the US$66.5b recorded a year earlier, which helps explain why trailing EPS has turned from a loss of US$18.36 to a profit of US$2.49 per share.
    • At the same time, the fact that forecast revenue growth is slower than the broader market keeps the focus on how efficiently Boeing converts this larger sales base into earnings, not just on top line expansion alone.
To see how this revenue trend fits into a full story on growth, risks, and valuation, check the balanced narrative readers are following for Boeing. 📊 Read the full Boeing Consensus Narrative.

Valuation signals vs US$244.56 share price

  • With the share price around US$244.56, Boeing is trading at a P/S of 2.3x compared to peer and industry averages of 3.5x and 3.8x, and below a DCF fair value estimate of about US$338.39, while analysts also expect earnings to grow about 29.13% per year and for the company to become profitable within three years.
  • What challenges a purely cautious stance is that these valuation and growth figures sit alongside the recent move from a trailing twelve month loss of US$11.7b at Q1 2025 to a trailing profit of US$1.9b at Q4 2025, so the bearish focus on past losses now has to be weighed against both improving trailing profitability and the projected 29.13% annual earnings growth.
    • The gap between the current share price of US$244.56 and the DCF fair value of roughly US$338.39 raises a question about whether the market is fully reflecting those earnings forecasts or still anchored to the earlier loss making periods.
    • At the same time, the risk summary notes that losses have increased over the past five years and that revenue growth is expected to be slower than the US market, which gives bears concrete reasons to question how quickly that implied upside might be realized.

data.title: Crane (CR) Margin Expansion To 14.4% Tests Bearish Premium Valuation Narratives
data.description:
Crane (CR) has put solid numbers on the table for FY 2025, with Q4 revenue of US$581 million and basic EPS of US$1.42 anchoring a year in which trailing twelve month revenue reached US$2.3 billion and EPS came in at US$5.77. The company has seen revenue move from US$2.1 billion and EPS of US$4.69 on a trailing basis at the end of 2024 to US$2.3 billion and EPS of US$5.77 by Q4 2025, alongside earnings growth of 23.7% and net profit margin stepping up from 12.6% to 14.4%. This sets a clear backdrop of improving profitability for investors to weigh against the latest release.

See our full analysis for Crane.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely followed narratives around Crane, where some long held views on growth, quality and risk may be reinforced while others are put to the test.

Advertisement

23.7% earnings jump against flat five year trend

  • On a trailing basis, earnings grew 23.7% over the last year while the five year annualized change is roughly flat at about 0% per year. This indicates that most of the progress is coming from the recent twelve month stretch rather than a long upward trend.
  • For a bullish view, what stands out is that this stronger year is backed by concrete numbers, with trailing net income at US$331.7 million and basic EPS at US$5.77. This supports the idea of a higher quality earnings base, although the flat five year profile is a reminder that this improvement has not been a long running pattern.
    • Bulls pointing to operating strength can reference the rise in trailing revenue to about US$2.3b alongside that 23.7% earnings lift, which ties absolute profit growth to a larger top line.
    • At the same time, critics of the bullish angle can point out that a flat five year earnings trend means this recent step up has not yet translated into a long streak of compounding results.
To see how this recent earnings jump fits into the longer term story some investors are watching, you can read the broader narrative now. 📊 Read the full Crane Consensus Narrative.

Margins at 14.4% change the earnings mix

  • Net profit margin on the trailing numbers sits at 14.4% compared with 12.6% a year earlier. That margin is built on trailing revenue of about US$2.3b and net income of US$331.7 million, so more of each sales dollar is turning into profit than in the prior period.
  • For the bullish angle, an interesting point is how this higher margin interacts with the quarterly pattern. Q4 2025 revenue of US$581 million and net income of US$81.7 million compare with Q3 2025 revenue of US$589.2 million and net income of US$91.4 million, suggesting the full year margin improvement is not just about one unusually strong quarter.
    • Supporters of the bullish story can highlight that across the four quarters of 2025, net income excluding extra items stayed between US$78.3 million and US$91.4 million per quarter while revenue ranged from US$557.6 million to US$589.2 million. This is consistent with the higher trailing margin.
    • More cautious investors might point out that some of the trailing profit profile also includes earnings from discontinued operations, which totalled US$34.9 million on a trailing basis and appeared in several 2025 quarters. Separating ongoing from discontinued contributions is important when judging how durable the 14.4% margin is.

P/E of 32.2x with DCF fair value above price

  • What creates tension for a bearish narrative is that skeptics often focus on the premium multiple and slower forecasts, with revenue expected to grow 7.1% a year and earnings 12.2% a year versus US market forecasts of 10.6% and 16.1% respectively. The same data set also shows the price sitting below the DCF fair value and backed by a 23.7% trailing earnings lift and a 14.4% net margin.
    • Bears arguing that the current P/E is stretched can point to those below market growth forecasts and the five year flat earnings trend, which together raise questions about how much future growth is being paid for at 32.2x earnings.
    • Investors pushing back on that cautious view can note that the combination of a price below the stated DCF fair value and the higher trailing margin suggests the premium multiple is at least partly anchored in recent fundamentals rather than only in optimistic expectations.

51.7% earnings growth versus five year decline

  • On a trailing basis, earnings are up 51.7% year over year to US$14.8 million of net income and US$1.90 EPS, while the five year history shows earnings declining about 12.1% per year. The latest jump therefore sits against a weaker longer run trend.
  • What stands out for a bullish take is that the current 16.4% net profit margin and US$90.4 million in trailing revenue sit alongside that 51.7% earnings growth. Critics can point to the five year decline to argue this strength may be more of a rebound than a long term pattern.

    • On the cautious side, the same investors may point out that analysts expect earnings growth of about 6.1% per year while revenue is forecast to decline about 6.1% per year, which creates a tension between the current valuation and how the top line might behave over the next few years.

data.title: China Lesso Group Holdings (HKG:2128) shareholders notch a 75% return over 1 year, yet earnings have been shrinking
data.description:
These days it's easy to simply buy an index fund, and your returns should (roughly) match the market. But you can significantly boost your returns by picking above-average stocks. For example, the China Lesso Group Holdings Limited (HKG:2128) share price is up 66% in the last 1 year, clearly besting the market return of around 31% (not including dividends). That's a solid performance by our standards! In contrast, the longer term returns are negative, since the share price is 37% lower than it was three years ago.

After a strong gain in the past week, it's worth seeing if longer term returns have been driven by improving fundamentals.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

To quote Buffett, 'Ships will sail around the world but the Flat Earth Society will flourish. There will continue to be wide discrepancies between price and value in the marketplace...' One imperfect but simple way to consider how the market perception of a company has shifted is to compare the change in the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price movement.

Over the last twelve months, China Lesso Group Holdings actually shrank its EPS by 12%.

Given the share price gain, we doubt the market is measuring progress with EPS. Therefore, it seems likely that investors are putting more weight on metrics other than EPS, at the moment.

Unfortunately China Lesso Group Holdings' fell 11% over twelve months. So the fundamental metrics don't provide an obvious explanation for the share price gain.

You can see below how earnings and revenue have changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

We consider it positive that insiders have made significant purchases in the last year. Even so, future earnings will be far more important to whether current shareholders make money. So it makes a lot of sense to check out what analysts think China Lesso Group Holdings will earn in the future (free profit forecasts).

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. So for companies that pay a generous dividend, the TSR is often a lot higher than the share price return. We note that for China Lesso Group Holdings the TSR over the last 1 year was 75%, which is better than the share price return mentioned above. The dividends paid by the company have thusly boosted the total shareholder return.

A Different Perspective

It's good to see that China Lesso Group Holdings has rewarded shareholders with a total shareholder return of 75% in the last twelve months. And that does include the dividend. There's no doubt those recent returns are much better than the TSR loss of 8% per year over five years. This makes us a little wary, but the business might have turned around its fortunes. I find it very interesting to look at share price over the long term as a proxy for business performance. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. To that end, you should learn about the 2 warning signs we've spotted with China Lesso Group Holdings (including 1 which makes us a bit uncomfortable) .

data.title: Eleco's (LON:ELCO) three-year earnings growth trails the 28% YoY shareholder returns
data.description:
The most you can lose on any stock (assuming you don't use leverage) is 100% of your money. But when you pick a company that is really flourishing, you can make more than 100%. For instance the Eleco plc (LON:ELCO) share price is 101% higher than it was three years ago. That sort of return is as solid as granite. And in the last week the share price has popped 16%.

Since it's been a strong week for Eleco shareholders, let's have a look at trend of the longer term fundamentals.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

To paraphrase Benjamin Graham: Over the short term the market is a voting machine, but over the long term it's a weighing machine. By comparing earnings per share (EPS) and share price changes over time, we can get a feel for how investor attitudes to a company have morphed over time.

Eleco was able to grow its EPS at 17% per year over three years, sending the share price higher. In comparison, the 26% per year gain in the share price outpaces the EPS growth. This suggests that, as the business progressed over the last few years, it gained the confidence of market participants. It's not unusual to see the market 're-rate' a stock, after a few years of growth.

You can see below how EPS has changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

We consider it positive that insiders have made significant purchases in the last year. Having said that, most people consider earnings and revenue growth trends to be a more meaningful guide to the business. This free interactive report on Eleco's earnings, revenue and cash flow is a great place to start, if you want to investigate the stock further.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. So for companies that pay a generous dividend, the TSR is often a lot higher than the share price return. In the case of Eleco, it has a TSR of 107% for the last 3 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. And there's no prize for guessing that the dividend payments largely explain the divergence!

A Different Perspective

Eleco provided a TSR of 2.0% over the last twelve months. But that was short of the market average. It's probably a good sign that the company has an even better long term track record, having provided shareholders with an annual TSR of 10% over five years. Maybe the share price is just taking a breather while the business executes on its growth strategy. If you want to research this stock further, the data on insider buying is an obvious place to start. You can click here to see who has been buying shares - and the price they paid.

Eleco is not the only stock insiders are buying. So take a peek at this free list of small cap companies at attractive valuations which insiders have been buying.

data.title: Calculating The Intrinsic Value Of Goodwin PLC (LON:GDWN)
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Goodwin's estimated fair value is UK£314 based on 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity
  • Current share price of UK£261 suggests Goodwin is potentially trading close to its fair value
  • Peers of Goodwin are currently trading on average at a 19% premium

How far off is Goodwin PLC (LON:GDWN) from its intrinsic value? Using the most recent financial data, we'll take a look at whether the stock is fairly priced by taking the forecast future cash flows of the company and discounting them back to today's value. One way to achieve this is by employing the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model. Before you think you won't be able to understand it, just read on! It's actually much less complex than you'd imagine.

We would caution that there are many ways of valuing a company and, like the DCF, each technique has advantages and disadvantages in certain scenarios. Anyone interested in learning a bit more about intrinsic value should have a read of the Simply Wall St analysis model.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Crunching The Numbers

We are going to use a two-stage DCF model, which, as the name states, takes into account two stages of growth. The first stage is generally a higher growth period which levels off heading towards the terminal value, captured in the second 'steady growth' period. To begin with, we have to get estimates of the next ten years of cash flows. Seeing as no analyst estimates of free cash flow are available to us, we have extrapolate the previous free cash flow (FCF) from the company's last reported value. We assume companies with shrinking free cash flow will slow their rate of shrinkage, and that companies with growing free cash flow will see their growth rate slow, over this period. We do this to reflect that growth tends to slow more in the early years than it does in later years.

A DCF is all about the idea that a dollar in the future is less valuable than a dollar today, so we discount the value of these future cash flows to their estimated value in today's dollars:

10-year free cash flow (FCF) estimate

2026202720282029203020312032203320342035
Levered FCF (£, Millions) UK£67.9mUK£87.6mUK£106.2mUK£122.9mUK£137.6mUK£150.3mUK£161.3mUK£171.1mUK£179.9mUK£187.9m
Growth Rate Estimate SourceEst @ 40.15%Est @ 29.00%Est @ 21.20%Est @ 15.73%Est @ 11.91%Est @ 9.23%Est @ 7.36%Est @ 6.05%Est @ 5.13%Est @ 4.49%
Present Value (£, Millions) Discounted @ 8.6% UK£62.6UK£74.3UK£83.0UK£88.4UK£91.1UK£91.7UK£90.7UK£88.5UK£85.7UK£82.5

("Est" = FCF growth rate estimated by Simply Wall St)
Present Value of 10-year Cash Flow (PVCF) = UK£839m

The second stage is also known as Terminal Value, this is the business's cash flow after the first stage. For a number of reasons a very conservative growth rate is used that cannot exceed that of a country's GDP growth. In this case we have used the 5-year average of the 10-year government bond yield (3.0%) to estimate future growth. In the same way as with the 10-year 'growth' period, we discount future cash flows to today's value, using a cost of equity of 8.6%.

Terminal Value (TV)= FCF2035 × (1 + g) ÷ (r – g) = UK£188m× (1 + 3.0%) ÷ (8.6%– 3.0%) = UK£3.5b

Present Value of Terminal Value (PVTV)= TV / (1 + r)10= UK£3.5b÷ ( 1 + 8.6%)10= UK£1.5b

The total value, or equity value, is then the sum of the present value of the future cash flows, which in this case is UK£2.4b. To get the intrinsic value per share, we divide this by the total number of shares outstanding. Relative to the current share price of UK£261, the company appears about fair value at a 17% discount to where the stock price trades currently. The assumptions in any calculation have a big impact on the valuation, so it is better to view this as a rough estimate, not precise down to the last cent.

The Assumptions

Now the most important inputs to a discounted cash flow are the discount rate, and of course, the actual cash flows. If you don't agree with these result, have a go at the calculation yourself and play with the assumptions. The DCF also does not consider the possible cyclicality of an industry, or a company's future capital requirements, so it does not give a full picture of a company's potential performance. Given that we are looking at Goodwin as potential shareholders, the cost of equity is used as the discount rate, rather than the cost of capital (or weighted average cost of capital, WACC) which accounts for debt. In this calculation we've used 8.6%, which is based on a levered beta of 1.096. Beta is a measure of a stock's volatility, compared to the market as a whole. We get our beta from the industry average beta of globally comparable companies, with an imposed limit between 0.8 and 2.0, which is a reasonable range for a stable business.

View our latest analysis for Goodwin

SWOT Analysis for Goodwin

Strength

data.title: Return Trends At Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia (BVMF:EQMA3B) Aren't Appealing
data.description:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. So, when we ran our eye over Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's (BVMF:EQMA3B) trend of ROCE, we liked what we saw.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.13 = R$1.2b ÷ (R$12b - R$2.3b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has an ROCE of 13%. That's a relatively normal return on capital, and it's around the 12% generated by the Electric Utilities industry.

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

While the returns on capital are good, they haven't moved much. The company has employed 59% more capital in the last five years, and the returns on that capital have remained stable at 13%. 13% is a pretty standard return, and it provides some comfort knowing that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has consistently earned this amount. Stable returns in this ballpark can be unexciting, but if they can be maintained over the long run, they often provide nice rewards to shareholders.

The Key Takeaway

The main thing to remember is that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has proven its ability to continually reinvest at respectable rates of return. However, over the last five years, the stock has only delivered a 24% return to shareholders who held over that period. That's why it could be worth your time looking into this stock further to discover if it has more traits of a multi-bagger.

data.title: We Think Empreendimentos Pague Menos (BVMF:PGMN3) Is Taking Some Risk With Its Debt
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. We can see that Empreendimentos Pague Menos S.A. (BVMF:PGMN3) does use debt in its business. But the more important question is: how much risk is that debt creating?

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more frequent (but still costly) occurrence is where a company must issue shares at bargain-basement prices, permanently diluting shareholders, just to shore up its balance sheet. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

How Much Debt Does Empreendimentos Pague Menos Carry?

As you can see below, at the end of September 2025, Empreendimentos Pague Menos had R$1.75b of debt, up from R$1.46b a year ago. Click the image for more detail. However, it also had R$106.3m in cash, and so its net debt is R$1.64b.

How Strong Is Empreendimentos Pague Menos' Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Empreendimentos Pague Menos had liabilities of R$3.28b due within a year, and liabilities of R$3.13b falling due after that. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of R$106.3m as well as receivables valued at R$1.45b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by R$4.85b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of R$4.25b, we think shareholders really should watch Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

See our latest analysis for Empreendimentos Pague Menos

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). This way, we consider both the absolute quantum of the debt, as well as the interest rates paid on it.

While Empreendimentos Pague Menos has a quite reasonable net debt to EBITDA multiple of 2.4, its interest cover seems weak, at 1.7. This does suggest the company is paying fairly high interest rates. In any case, it's safe to say the company has meaningful debt. Importantly, Empreendimentos Pague Menos grew its EBIT by 37% over the last twelve months, and that growth will make it easier to handle its debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But it is future earnings, more than anything, that will determine Empreendimentos Pague Menos's ability to maintain a healthy balance sheet going forward. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So we clearly need to look at whether that EBIT is leading to corresponding free cash flow. Over the last three years, Empreendimentos Pague Menos reported free cash flow worth 19% of its EBIT, which is really quite low. For us, cash conversion that low sparks a little paranoia about is ability to extinguish debt.

Our View

On the face of it, Empreendimentos Pague Menos's level of total liabilities left us tentative about the stock, and its interest cover was no more enticing than the one empty restaurant on the busiest night of the year. But on the bright side, its EBIT growth rate is a good sign, and makes us more optimistic. Once we consider all the factors above, together, it seems to us that Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt is making it a bit risky. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but we'd generally feel more comfortable with less leverage. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. We've identified 4 warning signs with Empreendimentos Pague Menos (at least 2 which are a bit concerning) , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

At the end of the day, it's often better to focus on companies that are free from net debt. You can access our special list of such companies (all with a track record of profit growth). It's free.
